food_subgroup: Noodle dish with grilled meat and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indomie noodles with chicken satay. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Indomie with chicken satay as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite noodle-satay dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Indomie noodles with chicken satay is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of cooked Indomie noodles (around 200 g), three to four chicken satay skewers (approximately 120 g), and a generous serving of peanut sauce (approximately 60 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from: (1) USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (FDC ID 20113); (2) USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 171477); (3) USDA FoodData Central — Peanut sauce (FDC ID 1100612); (4) HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database — Satay, chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Indomie noodles with chicken satay as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination: cooked Indomie noodles (~53% of serving weight, using USDA chow mein noodle cooked values as proxy), grilled chicken satay (~32% of serving weight, using HPB Singapore chicken satay and USDA grilled chicken breast), and peanut sauce (~16% of serving weight, using USDA peanut sauce). Per-100g values are derived from this weighted composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Instant noodles, cooked (GI ~53); Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Indomie noodles with chicken satay as a composite dish. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for instant noodles (cooked), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The addition of protein-rich chicken satay and fat-rich peanut sauce is expected to lower the effective GI of the composite meal; therefore GI 53 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 20.5 × 3.8) / 100 ≈ 20.7 (using 380 g serving, 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from instant noodle seasoning packet and soy-based sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,824 mg per standard 380 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced seasoning or sauce can partially mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from refined instant noodles partially offset by protein and fat from chicken satay and peanut sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the refined instant noodles contribute a moderate glycemic load (approximately 20.7 per serving); pairing with the protein from chicken satay and fat from peanut sauce helps blunt the postprandial glucose response, but portion control and reduced seasoning are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from peanut sauce and processed noodle seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g); the peanut sauce contributes beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats, but the overall sodium load and processed nature of the instant noodles make regular consumption inadvisable for those managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indomie Noodles with Chicken Satay is a popular fusion dish served in Singapore, combining Indonesian-origin Indomie instant noodles with grilled chicken satay skewers and a rich, savoury peanut sauce. The dish is commonly found at Indonesian-Singaporean casual dining restaurants such as Dapur Penyet. The Indomie noodles are typically prepared by boiling and then stir-frying with the included seasoning packet, which contains soy sauce, chilli, and flavour enhancers, giving the noodles a characteristic umami-rich, slightly spicy flavour. The chicken satay consists of marinated chicken pieces threaded onto skewers and grilled over charcoal or a gas grill, producing a smoky, caramelised exterior with a tender interior. The peanut sauce is thick, slightly sweet, and savoury, made from ground roasted peanuts, palm sugar, soy sauce, and spices. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of approximately 10 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The protein content is notable due to the chicken satay component. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) due to the instant noodle seasoning packet and soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-moderate), based on the instant noodle component, with the protein and fat from satay and peanut sauce expected to moderate the postprandial glucose response. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurants serving it. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with peanut, gluten, or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite restaurant dish (Indomie noodles with chicken satay). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked noodles, grilled chicken, and peanut sauce, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore chicken satay data. GI is estimated from the instant noodle component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of approved sources (USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore) as no direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish is available. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portions for this dish type. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace levels from the processed noodle component; this is a conservative estimate. 4. Sodium content is high due to the Indomie seasoning packet; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on instant noodles (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and should be treated as approximate. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore source databases for the component foods used in this estimation.
